author: @marcisaacs9407
text: As a part time potter I thank you👌 I am also a tinkerer with stuff and have rebuilt many motors etc. The biggest question is what is the best lubricant for small bushing type motors in fans for example. Tried several and the 3 in 1 blue works the longest at a few months but then it’s take it apart again. Looks like bronze bushing with felts. So what’s better considering when new they run for years without any attention at all. Usually these are Chinese made today. Your thoughts would be greatly appreciated 🙏

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Very nice work Dr. Robinstien! Also thanks to your assistant Lukor, I am wondering if you could put a few stages of magnets and coils along the shaft to harvest some of the energy your using to run the motor. Should be room for 3-5 sets up the shaft. Basically anything you have sitting in one place with a rotating shaft / pole or whatnot can effectively give you something back, even a friction wheel on the bottom pulley can drive a smaller DC motor. 
Just takes a moment's thought to let the ideas flow thanks to you. You're an excellent mentor and an even better inspirational motivator.
Thank you my friend. Main stage of solar is done and my bench is almost sorted, just need to make a shelving unit with battery and inverter compartments a be ready to start filming. I have an idea for a quick starter video.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: Also an induction motor is greate for this application in that it is an asynchronous motor tolerant of degree of slip in that it works through a large range of slip between the rotor rpm and the mains freqency.

replies.author: @nil5221
replies.text: I don't think this is correct. Induction motors are definitely synchronous, designed to run at a single speed, depending on the supply AC frequency and the number of wound poles. The formula is frequency x 60 ÷ number of poles. Under load, there is a small slip tolerated but greater slip will result in rapid overheating. In the worst case, the start winding will kick in again...look out for smoke and burnt-out windings. The only way to controllably vary the motor run speed is to vary the  supply frequency which takes electronics.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@nil5221 Induction motors are asynchronous, as in, the AC freqency does not = rpm×2/(poles). Induction motors general running conditions are 2 to 5% slip with peek torque at around 10% slip, and torque drops as slip grows from above 10%, and can even generate breaking torque if the motor is run backwards from its rotating magnetic field.  Google induction machine (motor or generator) torque graph for generalized images of the torque curve. 

But I'll grant you that they are not ment to be run outside typical run conditions for long periods of time, certainly not at peek torque.  

 I should have been more clear with my more tolerant comment.  An asynchronous motor can get jumpy and sporadic (not good for spinning clay pots) when forced outside its synchronous speed. One may avoid this with a higher HP motor,  but an induction motor will gradually slip further and further generating more torque untill its peak and drop from there and recover easilly when the load is removed. 

1/4 HP is plenty sufficient torque for a potter's wheel.

replies.author: @nil5221
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 thank you for clarifying!
